original_abstract:
This study presents concurrent measurements of particle hygroscopic growth, cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition conducted during the HCCT-2010 field campaign in the Thüringer Wald mountain range, Germany. Using a Hygroscopicity Tandem Differential Mobility Analyzer (HTDMA), we observed a clear size dependence in particle water uptake, with median hygroscopicity parameters ($\kappa$) increasing from 0.14 for 50 nm particles to 0.28 for 250 nm particles. 

A predictive closure analysis based on the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) mixing rule demonstrated that while submicrometer bulk composition significantly overestimates hygroscopicity for smaller particles (by 30–40%), the use of size-resolved mass spectrometry data substantially improves the agreement. Discrepancies between measured and predicted values were partially attributed to the evaporation of semi-volatile ammonium nitrate within the HTDMA. Furthermore, we identified a positive correlation between the organic hygroscopicity parameter ($\kappa_{org}$) and the oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) ratio ($\kappa_{org} = 0.19 \times \text{O:C} - 0.03$), which served as a valuable constraint for the closure model. 

Comparing sub- and supersaturated regimes, $\kappa$ values derived from CCN measurements were approximately 30% higher than those derived from HTDMA data. This systematic difference likely arises from surface tension effects, solution non-ideality, and the co-condensation of semi-volatile compounds, suggesting that extrapolating hygroscopic properties across the activation threshold requires caution. Finally, we achieved a successful closure between CCN activity and chemical composition by applying CCN-specific hygroscopicity parameters for individual components, highlighting the importance of size-segregated chemical data in predicting cloud-forming potential.
humanized_abstract:
We report simultaneous measurements of particle hygroscopic growth factor (HGF), cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity and size resolved chemical composition of aerosol particles taken during the HCCT-2010 campaign at Thüringer Wald mountain range, Germany. Using an HTDMA, we found that aerosol particles showed strong dependence on particle size for water uptake, as indicated by the median hygroscopicity parameter (κ) values of 0.14 and 0.28 for 50 nm and 250 nm particles, respectively.

Using the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) model for the predictive closure analysis, we found that submicrometer bulk composition overestimates the hygroscopicity of smaller particles by 30–40%, while size-resolved mass spectrometry data improves the agreement. A portion of the discrepancies may be attributed to the evaporation of semi-volatile ammonium nitrate in the HTDMA. Furthermore, we identified a strong linear relationship between the organic hygroscopicity parameter and the oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) ratio: $\kappa_{org} = 0.19 \times \text{O:C} - 0.03$.

In comparison, the κ values derived from CCN observations were on average 30% higher than those derived from HTDMA data. The cause for this observation may be related to the influence of surface tension, solution non-ideality, as well as co-condensation of semi-volatile organics. This underlines the necessity of a sensitive approach when parameters measured under high supersaturation are extrapolated to low supersaturation (activation) conditions. With the help of CCN specific κ values for the composition derived from the size-segregated ACS data we finally reached closure between CCN activity and composition.

document.output:
We report simultaneous measurements of particle hygroscopic growth factor (HGF), cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity and size resolved chemical composition of aerosol particles taken during the HCCT-2010 campaign at Thüringer Wald mountain range, Germany. A portion of the discrepancies may be attributed to the evaporation of semi-volatile ammonium nitrate in the HTDMA. Furthermore, we identified a strong linear relationship between the organic hygroscopicity parameter and the oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) ratio: $\kappa_{org} = 0.19 \times \text{O:C} - 0.03$.

The cause for this observation may be related to the influence of surface tension, solution non-ideality, as well as co-condensation of semi-volatile organics. document.input:
This study presents concurrent measurements of particle hygroscopic growth, cloud condensation nuclei (CCN) activity, and size-resolved chemical composition conducted during the HCCT-2010 field campaign in the Thüringer Wald mountain range, Germany. Using a Hygroscopicity Tandem Differential Mobility Analyzer (HTDMA), we observed a clear size dependence in particle water uptake, with median hygroscopicity parameters ($\kappa$) increasing from 0.14 for 50 nm particles to 0.28 for 250 nm particles. 

A predictive closure analysis based on the Zdanovskii–Stokes–Robinson (ZSR) mixing rule demonstrated that while submicrometer bulk composition significantly overestimates hygroscopicity for smaller particles (by 30–40%), the use of size-resolved mass spectrometry data substantially improves the agreement. Discrepancies between measured and predicted values were partially attributed to the evaporation of semi-volatile ammonium nitrate within the HTDMA. Furthermore, we identified a positive correlation between the organic hygroscopicity parameter ($\kappa_{org}$) and the oxygen-to-carbon (O:C) ratio ($\kappa_{org} = 0.19 \times \text{O:C} - 0.03$), which served as a valuable constraint for the closure model. 

Comparing sub- and supersaturated regimes, $\kappa$ values derived from CCN measurements were approximately 30% higher than those derived from HTDMA data. This systematic difference likely arises from surface tension effects, solution non-ideality, and the co-condensation of semi-volatile compounds, suggesting that extrapolating hygroscopic properties across the activation threshold requires caution. Finally, we achieved a successful closure between CCN activity and chemical composition by applying CCN-specific hygroscopicity parameters for individual components, highlighting the importance of size-segregated chemical data in predicting cloud-forming potential.
